question: Aging and Health: How does the chronic disease burden evolve from age 45 onwards in this specific population?
prompt: I need a comprehensive Python analysis focused on chronic diseases and demographic factors using a Telemedicine dataset (a CSV file named 'Table1'). The analysis must be divided into three parts. For each part, generate a professional visualization, print a console report, and export the exact underlying data to a CSV file. Use pandas, numpy, matplotlib, seaborn, plotly, and scipy. Part 1: Evolution of Age by Chronic Disease (Ridgeline Plot). Data Processing: Focus on the 'Idade' (Age) column and all structured checkbox columns related to 'doenças crônicas' (chronic diseases). Aggregation: Propagate the age and chronic disease status across all patient records (forward fill) and keep only the last record per unique 'Record ID'. Melting & Cleaning: Reshape the data (melt) to have 'Doenca' and 'Status' columns. Keep only rows where 'Status' is 'Checked'. Clean the disease names (remove 'choice='). Crucially, exclude meaningless categories like 'não se aplica', 'nenhuma', 'outras', etc., using a RegEx exclusion pattern. Remove rows with missing age. Visualization: Select the Top 8 most frequent chronic diseases. Create an elegant Plotly Ridgeline Plot (Violin plot with side='positive') showing the age distribution for each disease. Set the background to solid white, use a bold qualitative color palette, add dark outlines to the violins, and include the mean line within each distribution. Reverse the order so the most frequent disease is at the top. Output: Generate a statistical summary table for these Top 8 diseases (Count, Min, Mean, Median, Max Age). Print it to the console and export it silently to 'Tabela_Ridgeline_Idades_DoencasCronicas.csv'. Part 2: Chronic Disease Burden and Aging (>45 years). Data Processing: Filter for actual consultations. Ensure 'Idade' is numeric and propagated correctly. Drop rows with missing age. Create a new column 'Grupo_Etario' (Age Group) classifying patients as '45 anos ou mais' (>= 45) or 'Menos de 45 anos' (< 45). Disease Tracking: Identify Hypertension (HAS) and Diabetes (DM) using both free-text mining (RegEx for terms like 'hipertensão', 'has', 'diabetes', 'dm2') and structured checkboxes. Combine these into binary columns ('Tem_HAS', 'Tem_DM'). Statistics: Use chi2_contingency from scipy.stats to calculate the p-value comparing the prevalence of HAS and DM between the two age groups. Output: Create a table containing the diagnosis (Hypertension/Diabetes), age group, total consultations, absolute cases, prevalence (%), and p-value. Print a detailed executive summary to the console showing these exact numbers and export the table silently to 'Tabela_Carga_Cronicas_Idade.csv'. Visualization: Create a Seaborn grouped bar chart showing the Prevalence (%) on the Y-axis and Diagnosis (HAS/DM) on the X-axis, grouped by Age Group. Use specific colors: Blue ('#4a90e2') for <45 and Terracotta ('#d55e52') for >=45. Add percentage labels directly on top of the bars and remove top/right spines. [cite: 51-71]

question: Operational Efficiency - Consultation Time: What is the average duration of tele-appointments by specialty, and which demand the most physician time?
prompt: I need an advanced data analysis and data-cleaning script in Python for a Telemedicine dataset (CSV file named 'Table1'). Use pandas, numpy, matplotlib, seaborn, and regular expressions (re). Part 1: Fail-Safe Time Column Detection and Cleaning. Build a robust 'fail-safe' detector function to identify the true column that stores the consultation time ('tempo', 'duração', 'minutos') while ignoring columns related to 'especialidade'. The function must handle messy data: extract numbers even if mixed with text (like 'h' or 'hora'), convert hours to minutes, and ignore empty strings, 'nan', 'unchecked', or '0'. Test candidate columns and select the one with the highest count of valid numeric time entries. Print a diagnostic message identifying the chosen column and its valid count. Calculate a Quality Indicator: The percentage of consultations that actually have the time filled in versus left blank. Print a management alert if more than 20% are blank. Part 2: Specialty Cross-Referencing (Real Data vs. Fallback). Cross-reference the valid time data with the medical specialties ('Para qual especialidade', 'choice='). For each consultation, match the filled time with the specific specialty requested and the CIAP-2 code. Fallback Mechanism: If the initial cross-reference yields an empty dataset (meaning time and specialty were filled in different forms/instances), automatically activate a 'Data Cascade' (forward fill grouped by 'Record ID') to align the data longitudinally and try the extraction again. Raise a fatal error if it still fails. Part 3: Outlier Mathematics (IQR Method). Apply the Interquartile Range (IQR) mathematical method to calculate the lower and upper bounds of normal consultation times. Separate the data into 'df_normal' (clean data) and 'df_outliers'. Calculate both the 'dirty mean' (with outliers) and the 'clean mean' (without outliers). Part 4: Visualization (Clean Violin Plot). Create a Seaborn Violin Plot showing the distribution of 'Tempo de Consulta (Minutos)' on the X-axis for the Top 10 most frequent specialties on the Y-axis. Crucially, use only the clean data without outliers (df_normal). The violins should use the 'plasma_r' color palette, be semi-transparent, and not show the inner boxplot. Overlay a scatterplot (stripplot) of the actual data points in black, and add a red diamond marker representing the mean for each specialty. Customize the Y-axis labels to include the specialty name (truncated at 25 characters), the sample size (n), and the exact median time in minutes. Sort the Y-axis descending by median time. Part 5: Exporting Data and Auditing Outliers. Export the exact statistical data used to build the chart (Count, Min, Mean, Median, Max, Standard Deviation for the clean data of the Top 10 specialties) to a CSV named 'Tabela_Estatistica_Tempo_Especialidade.csv'. Print a detailed Executive Report to the console. It must include the Quality Indicator results, the Outlier Audit (comparing the clean vs. dirty mean), and specifically list the Top 3 Specialties and Top 3 CIAP diagnoses that caused the extremely long outlier consultations (those above the upper IQR limit). [cite: 87-103]

question: First Contact Resolution: What is the proportion of patients who resolve their demand in the first teleconsultation?
prompt: I need a Python script to analyze the 'Non-Resolution' reasons in the first contact of a Telemedicine service, using the dataset a CSV file named 'Table1'. The analysis must be divided into three parts. Use pandas, numpy, matplotlib, seaborn, and plotly. Part 1: Text Mining for Non-Resolution Reasons (Bar Chart). Data Processing: Search the free-text clinical notes columns (like 'conduta', 'evolucao', 'plano') for specific keywords that indicate the issue was not resolved in the primary care consultation. The categories and keywords are: 'Solicitação de Exames' (Keywords: 'exame', 'laborat', 'sangue', 'rx', 'raio-x', 'ultrassom', 'solicito'); 'Encaminhamento Presencial' (Keywords: 'presencial', 'ubs', 'upa', 'exame fisico', 'palpação'); 'Encaminhamento Especialista' (Keywords: 'especialista', 'encaminho', 'parecer', 'cardiologista'); 'Retorno Agendado' (Keywords: 'retorno', 'voltar', 'dias', 'reavaliar'). Visualization: Create a horizontal Bar Chart (Seaborn) showing the frequency of these 4 categories, sorted from highest to lowest. Use the 'flare' color palette. Display the absolute values directly at the end of each bar. Remove the top and right spines of the chart. Output: Print an executive diagnostic identifying the top limiting factor. Export a table with the Reason, Frequency, and Proportion (%) to a CSV file. Part 2: Funnel Classification and Sankey Diagram. Data Processing: Implement a decision funnel (hierarchy) to classify the 'Desfecho' (Outcome) of each consultation into one of the 4 non-resolution categories or 'Resolvido/Alta' (Resolved/Discharged). The priority order for classification should be: 1. Presencial, 2. Especialista, 3. Exames, 4. Retorno. If none match, classify as 'Resolvido/Alta'. Ignore entries with 'não compareceu' or very short text (<5 chars) as 'Outros'. Calculations: Calculate the total number of valid consultations, the number of 'Resolved' cases, and the number of 'Unresolved' cases (which is the sum of the 4 non-resolution categories). Visualization: Create a Sankey Diagram using Plotly to visualize this 'Mass Balance' flow. Node 0: Total Atendimentos; Node 1: Resolvido/Alta; Node 2: Não Resolvido; Nodes 3-6: The specific non-resolution reasons (Retorno, Exames, Especialista, Presencial). Include the absolute numbers (N) in the node labels. Output: Print a validation check in the terminal to ensure the numbers sum up correctly (Total = Resolved + Unresolved). Part 3: Generating the Sankey Base Data Table. Recreate the logic from Part 2 to establish the exact numerical values for the flow: 'Total' branching into 'Resolved' and 'Unresolved', and then 'Unresolved' branching into the 4 specific reasons. Output: Create a pandas DataFrame structured specifically for generating Sankey diagrams (Source, Target, Value). Print this table to the console and export the table as a CSV file named 'Table1.csv'. [cite: 114-127]

question: Gender Identity: Relationship between gender and clinical complexity.
prompt: Act as an expert Python Data Scientist and Epidemiologist. I need a robust Python script to analyze the relationship between 'Gender Identity' and 'Clinical Complexity' in a Telemedicine dataset. Environment & Setup: File: Read from a CSV named Table1 using low_memory=False. Libraries: Use pandas, numpy, matplotlib.pyplot, and seaborn. Robustness: Dynamically identify columns using partial string matching for Gender Identity ('identidade de gênero'), Biological Sex ('sexo'), Infectious Diseases, Chronic Diseases, Specialties, and CIAP-2. Please divide the code into the following sections: Part 1: Data Cleaning & Smart Gender Imputation. Longitudinal Fill: Sort by Patient ID and Instance, then forward-fill the demographic and clinical columns so history is preserved across consultations. Smart Imputation: Create a function to infer the patient's group. If the 'Gender Identity' field is missing, 'NaN', or 'Prefers not to say', infer it from the 'Biological Sex' column (e.g., Male -> 'Homem Cis', Female -> 'Mulher Cis'). If explicitly stated, map them correctly to 'Mulher Cis', 'Homem Cis', 'Mulher Trans', and 'Homem Trans'. Drop unmapped rows. Complexity Calculation: Calculate the 'Total Disease Burden' per consultation by summing all marked infectious and chronic disease checkboxes. Part 2: Specialty Filtering & Exporting. Melting: Melt the Specialties columns. Targeting: Identify the Top 3 most requested specialties for each gender group. Combine these into a unique target list, and explicitly force the inclusion of 'Psiquiatria/Saúde Mental' (Psychiatry) in this list if it isn't there already. Outputs: Export a statistical summary (Mean, Std, Count of Disease Burden grouped by Specialty and Gender) to base_complexidade_genero.csv. Export the Top 5 CIAP-2 reasons for each gender (with absolute frequencies and proportions) to base_ciap_por_genero.csv. Print the Top 3 CIAP-2 reasons per gender to the console. Part 3: Visualization. Create a large horizontal grouped Bar Plot (sns.barplot). Axes: Specialty on the Y-axis, Total Disease Burden on the X-axis. Group by Gender Identity (hue). Styling: Use a custom palette: Cis Women (Gray #95a5a6), Cis Men (Dark Blue #34495e), Trans Women (Purple #9b59b6), Trans Men (Orange #e67e22). Add error bars (capsize=.1). Remove top and right spines. [cite: 154-172]

question: Respiratory Seasonality: Correlation between respiratory diseases and climate temperature.
prompt: Act as an expert Python Data Scientist and Epidemiologist. I need a robust Python script to perform a time-series analysis correlating the seasonality of Respiratory Diseases with the average climate temperature in a Telemedicine dataset. Environment & Setup: File: Read from a CSV named TELESAPFormulrioMdic-EXPORTAOBOLSISTAAtua_DATA_LABELS_2025-07-23_1329.csv using low_memory=False. Libraries: Use pandas, numpy, matplotlib.pyplot, seaborn, and scipy.stats.chisquare. Robustness: Dynamically identify the date/timestamp column. Handle missing dates by dropping invalid rows. Dynamically identify text columns and checkboxes for respiratory conditions. Please divide the code into four distinct phases: Phase 1: Time-Series Extraction & Clinical Mining. Temporal Parsing: Dynamically locate the column containing 'data', 'timestamp', or 'date'. Convert it to datetime and extract the numerical Month. Respiratory Mining: Identify cases of Asthma, COPD, or Bronchitis. Use a hybrid approach: Text Mining: Search free-text columns using regex (e.g., \b(asma|dpoc|bronquite|doença pulmonar obstrutiva)\b). Structured Data: Search checkbox columns containing 'asma' or 'dpoc'. Binarization: Unify the findings into a single binary column Caso_Respiratorio (1 if any condition is met, 0 otherwise). Phase 2: Climate Integration & Statistical Testing. Monthly Aggregation: Group the data by Month (1 to 12). Calculate the Total Consultations, the Absolute Number of Respiratory Cases, and the % Rate of Respiratory Cases per month. Ensure all 12 months are represented (fill missing months with 0). Climate Mapping: Hardcode a dictionary with the historical average temperatures for São Paulo for each month (Jan: 23.1, Feb: 23.5, Mar: 22.5, Apr: 21.2, May: 18.4, Jun: 17.5, Jul: 16.6, Aug: 17.7, Sep: 19.3, Oct: 20.5, Nov: 21.6, Dec: 22.4) and map it to the aggregated dataframe. Chi-Square Test: Perform a Chi-Square test (chisquare) comparing the observed respiratory cases against the expected cases (assuming a uniform distribution across the total volume). Determine if the seasonal variance is statistically significant (p < 0.05). Phase 3: Data Export. Export 1: Export the monthly seasonal dataframe (Month, Total Consultations, Respiratory Cases, % Rate, Average SP Temp) to base_sazonalidade_respiratoria.csv. Export 2: Export the exact results of the Chi-Square test (Total N, Chi-Square Stat, P-Value, and Text Interpretation) to base_estatistica_sazonalidade.csv. Phase 4: Dual-Axis Visualization & Executive Report. Visualization: Create a Dual-Axis chart (ax1 and ax1.twinx()). Primary Y-Axis (Bar Chart): Plot the % Rate of Respiratory Cases as vertical bars in Blue (#3498db). Annotate the exact percentages on top of the bars. Secondary Y-Axis (Line Chart): Plot the Average SP Temperature as a Line with markers in Red (#e74c3c). Crucially, force the Y-limit of this secondary axis to start at 0 and end at 30 to provide a stable visual scale. Add the Statistical interpretation and p-value dynamically into the chart title. Color the title text Red if significant, Gray if not. Console Report: Print an Epidemiological Synthesis to the terminal. Include the Total N, total respiratory episodes, and the statistical diagnosis. If the variation is significant (p < 0.05), trigger a 'Management Alert' indicating the peak month, its proportion of the demand, and the climate temperature of that specific month.

question: Cardiovascular Risk: Stratification and patient profile analysis.
prompt: Act as an expert Python Data Scientist and Public Health Analyst. I need a robust Python script to perform a Cardiovascular Risk (RCV) stratification analysis on a Telemedicine dataset. Environment & Setup: File: Read from a CSV named Table1 using low_memory=False. Libraries: Use pandas, numpy, matplotlib.pyplot, and seaborn. Robustness: Dynamically identify text columns and structured checkbox columns for chronic diseases. Protect against NaN values during text mining. Please divide the code into four distinct phases: Phase 1: Clinical Text Mining and Patient Consolidation. Feature Engineering: Track three specific comorbidities: Hypertension (HAS), Diabetes (DM), and Dyslipidemia/Cholesterol (DLP). Hybrid Mining: For each disease, check both the free-text clinical notes using Regex (e.g., \b(hipertens[aã]o|has)\b, \b(diabetes|dm2)\b, \b(colesterol|dlp)\b) and the structured 'Doenças Crônicas' checkbox columns. Create three binary columns (Tem_HAS, Tem_DM, Tem_DLP). Consolidation: Group the data by unique Patient ID (Record ID) using .max() so that if a patient ever presented the comorbidity in their lifetime history, they are flagged as 1. Phase 2: Cardiovascular Risk (RCV) Stratification. Clinical Logic: Create a new column Estratificacao_RCV classifying each unique patient into one of three tiers based on the following medical rules: Alto Risco (High Risk): Patient has Diabetes (DM) OR presents a combination of Hypertension + Dyslipidemia (HAS + DLP). Risco Moderado (Moderate Risk): Patient has isolated Hypertension (HAS) OR isolated Dyslipidemia (DLP), but no Diabetes. Baixo Risco (Low Risk): Patient has none of the mapped factors. Phase 3: Data Export. Export 1 (Stratification): Count the absolute number and calculate the proportion (%) of patients in each of the 3 risk tiers. Export this to base_estatificacao_rcv.csv. Export 2 (High-Risk Profile): Isolate the 'Alto Risco' cohort and calculate its internal composition: how many patients are there due to Diabetes vs. how many are there purely due to the dangerous HAS + DLP combination. Export this breakdown to base_perfil_alto_risco_rcv.csv. Phase 4: Visualization & Executive Report. Visualization: Create a horizontal Bar chart (sns.barplot) mapping the Cardiovascular Risk Level on the Y-axis and the Number of Unique Patients on the X-axis. Styling: Use a 'traffic light' color palette reflecting clinical urgency: Red (#c0392b) for High Risk, Orange (#f39c12) for Moderate Risk, and Green (#2ecc71) for Low Risk. Add exact N and % annotations directly on the bars. Remove the top and right spines. Console Report: Print an executive diagnostic report to the terminal summarizing the population's RCV. Include a specific sub-section detailing the clinical profile of the High-Risk group (Total Diabetics vs. Total HAS+DLP combinations). [cite: 272-286]
